metadata.title: Fixed Broken References — Source Annotation Record
metadata.annotation_policy: All external statutes not in the stage-1 corpus receive a standard annotation noting their status. Content remains valid — these are real laws correctly cited — but the training system cannot perform automated cross-reference validation against them. Guideline cross-references are used to confirm factual accuracy.

fixed_references.annotation: source: US statute (FDA Amendments Act of 2007, Section 505-1 — Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies), not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: 21 CFR 314.81(b)(2)(i) (REMS post-marketing reporting) and FDA Guidance 'Format and Content of Proposed Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies' (2009). The statutory basis is correct; FDAAA §505-1 codified REMS requirements now reflected in 21 CFR Part 314.

source: US statute (21 USC §360cc — Orphan Drug Act designation and market exclusivity), not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: 21 CFR 316.31 (7-year orphan drug exclusivity), FDA Guidance 'Orphan Drug Regulations' (2013). The statute is correctly cited; 21 USC §360cc grants 7-year market exclusivity for orphan drugs, implemented via 21 CFR Part 316.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: Confirmed via 21 CFR 316 and FDA Orphan Drug Guidance cross-reference

fixed_references.annotation: source: US statute (21 USC §355-1 — REMS authority under FDAAA), not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: 21 CFR 314.81(b)(2)(i) and FDA REMS guidance documents. The statutory citation is correct; 21 USC §355-1 authorizes FDA to require REMS for certain drugs based on safety considerations. Implemented through 21 CFR Part 314 post-marketing reporting requirements.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: Confirmed via 21 CFR 314 and FDA REMS Guidance cross-reference

fixed_references.annotation: source: US state-level pharmacy laws (varies by state — outside federal regulatory scope), not in training corpus. This reference applies to drug substitution rules that vary by state pharmacy practice acts. At the federal level, the relevant framework is 21 CFR Part 201 (labeling) and FDA's interchangeability designation process under 21 USC §355(j). State substitution laws operate in parallel to FDA interchangeability designations. Training data cannot validate specific state law citations; the general principle that state laws govern retail substitution is correct.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: General principle confirmed via 21 CFR 201 and FDA biosimilar interchangeability guidance; specific state laws not validated
fixed_references.residual_risk: low — general principle is correct; specific state law provisions vary and cannot be validated from corpus

fixed_references.annotation: source: US statute (21 USC §360bb — Orphan Drug Act designation authority), not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: 21 CFR 316.20 (orphan drug designation application) and FDA Guidance 'Developing Products for Rare Diseases and Conditions' (2015). The citation is correct; 21 USC §360bb authorizes FDA to designate drugs for rare diseases or conditions (orphan designation). Implemented through 21 CFR Part 316.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: Confirmed via 21 CFR 316 and FDA rare disease guidance cross-reference

fixed_references.annotation: source: US statutes (21 USC §356(c) — Accelerated Approval authority; FDORA §3210 — FDA Omnibus Reform Act of 2022, accelerated approval post-market study requirements), not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: 21 CFR 314.510 (accelerated approval via surrogate endpoints), 21 CFR 601.41, and FDA Guidance 'Accelerated Approval of Drugs and Biologics' (2023). FDORA §3210 strengthened FDA's authority to withdraw accelerated approval if post-market studies fail to confirm benefit. Both citations are factually correct and implemented through 21 CFR 314.510/601.41.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: Confirmed via 21 CFR 314.510, 601.41, and FDA Accelerated Approval Guidance cross-reference

fixed_references.annotation: source: US statute (21 USC §356c — discontinuation or supply interruption of life-saving drugs), not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: 21 CFR 314.81(b)(3)(iii) (post-marketing discontinuation reporting) and FDA Guidance 'Drug Shortage Reporting' (2013). The citation is correct; 21 USC §356c requires manufacturers to notify FDA at least 6 months before discontinuing a life-saving drug. The notification requirements are implemented through FDA's drug shortage system.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: Confirmed via 21 CFR 314.81 and FDA Drug Shortage Guidance cross-reference

fixed_references.original_source_ref: Canadian Environmental Protection Act and CEPA notification requirements
fixed_references.annotation: source: Canadian Environmental Protection Act (CEPA 1999) and associated notification requirements, not in training corpus. Verified via guideline cross-reference: Health Canada Guidance 'Environmental Assessment of Drug Submissions' and the New Substances Notification Regulations (Chemicals and Polymers) under CEPA. For pharmaceutical submissions, the relevant pathway is typically the Environmental Assessment component of the Health Canada drug review process; most drug substances are excluded from CEPA notification via the Domestic Substances List (DSL). The regulatory basis is correctly cited; specific CEPA notification requirements apply when an API is not on the DSL.
fixed_references.corpus_validation: General framework confirmed via Health Canada Environmental Assessment Guidance; specific CEPA provisions not validated from corpus
fixed_references.residual_risk: low — framework is correct; product-specific DSL status and CEPA applicability cannot be determined from corpus alone

us_statute_21usc.verification_method: 21 CFR cross-reference and FDA guidance documents

state_law.verification_method: General principle confirmed; specific state laws outside corpus scope

canadian_statute.verification_method: Framework confirmed via Health Canada guidance; product-specific CEPA applicability outside corpus

summary.standard_annotation: source: [jurisdiction] statute, not in training corpus, verified via guideline cross-reference
summary.recommendation: Add 21 USC Title 21 (United States Code) and CEPA texts to stage-2 corpus to enable full statute-level tracing and eliminate this class of broken reference.
